chunk_summary: Micronutrient monitoring in post-NEC and intestinal resection survivors focuses on addressing malabsorption, growth failure, and specific deficiencies (e.g., B12, fat-soluble vitamins) through structured surveillance and phenotype-based assessments post-discharge.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of micronutrient monitoring in post-NEC and intestinal resection survivors.
Identify risk factors for specific deficiencies based on intestinal anatomy and symptoms.
Learn appropriate surveillance intervals and clinical proxies for monitoring malabsorption.
Recognize when to refer to pediatric gastroenterology or intestinal rehabilitation teams.
key_facts: Ileal resection increases the risk of vitamin B12 deficiency and bile acid malabsorption.
Extensive small-bowel loss raises risks of fat-soluble vitamin (A/D/E/K) deficiency, zinc loss, and electrolyte depletion.
High stool output or watery stools can lead to chronic sodium and water losses, mimicking poor feeding.
Periodic checks every 1–3 months are recommended for high-risk infants post-discharge.
Clinical proxies for malabsorption include persistent diarrhea, poor linear growth, perianal rash, and recurrent dehydration.
Human milk is preferred, with fortification or specialized formulas as needed, but formula cycling without assessment is discouraged.
reference_quote: Ileal resection increases risk of vitamin B12 deficiency and bile acid malabsorption; extensive small-bowel loss increases risk of fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, zinc loss, and electrolyte depletion. (Source: University of Virginia GI Nutrition, Infants with NEC: nutritional management)
document_key_topics: Referral to pediatric gastroenterology or rehabilitation teams
